narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put significant effort into your application, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 and LSAT score of 26. Unfortunately, the university's machine learning model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 95.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—the school uses this tool to prioritize admits likely to pass on their first try and succeed in their legal careers. The model's decision was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school years 1 and 3, both at the lowest level (1st decile), which stood out sharply against the typical student's mid-range performance around the 5th or 6th decile. These early and later school grades had the strongest negative influence, pushing the prediction heavily toward failure because they signal potential challenges in handling rigorous legal coursework and exam preparation. Your LSAT score ranked next in influence, as its below-average level compared to the usual 37 further reinforced concerns about your readiness for the bar. While your undergraduate GPA, though lower than the typical 3.21, offered a small counterbalance by nudging the prediction slightly toward success, it wasn't enough to offset the top concerns. Your family income and full-time enrollment status provided minor positive boosts, aligning with common successful profiles, but they played smaller roles overall. In essence, compared to typical admitted students who excel in school deciles, LSAT, and GPA, your profile highlighted risks in those core academic areas that the model deemed too high. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character—many factors beyond these metrics shape success—but it explains the data-driven denial. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your growth and commitment. You're capable of turning this around, and with targeted improvements, stronger opportunities await. Keep pushing forward.
